query: ISO 6876-2001 ADA specifications 57 endodontics sealer penetration

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- ZOE Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: ZOE-based sealers are the most used sealers in clinical endodontics. They present as a powder-liquid or a 2-paste preparation. [5] Although ex vivo laboratory studies have demonstrated cytotoxic properties in ZOE sealers due to the presence of eugenol, they have proven to be clinically useful in both animal and human studies. [5] [9] Notable properties of ZOE sealers include absorption if extruded into the surrounding tissues and anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. [10] However, ZOE sealers become weak and porous upon setting, making them susceptible to decomposition if tissue fluids leak. [9] [10] Alternatively, other sealers that do not include eugenol are available and, therefore, mitigate any of its potentially deleterious effects. These sealers are aptly referred to as non-eugenol sealers.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: The role of the sealer is critical for establishing a fluid-tight seal of the root canal as it fills the vacant space between the prepared dentinal wall and the core filling material. [1] [5] It also reaches lateral canals and tubules inaccessible to gutta-percha cones and provides antimicrobial action. [5] Most sealers are toxic before setting, so care should be taken to avoid the extrusion of sealers into the periapical tissues. [6] Sealers induce different levels of periapical inflammation; however, this effect is usually transient and does not interfere with tissue healing. [7] Sealers are partly reabsorbed after exposure to tissue fluids. The core material should occupy almost all of the canal, and sealers should only be applied in small quantities. Several types of sealers are available, which vary in formulation and properties. They are classified into 5 main groups according to their composition: zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) based, calcium hydroxide based, glass ionomer-based, resin-based, and bioceramic sealers. The properties of an ideal sealer have been outlined in the literature. It is important to note that no material has been proven to eliminate leakage nor fulfill all the ideals.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- ZOE Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Absorption, if extruded past the apex Anti-inflammatory effects Antimicrobial effects [5] [9] Disadvantages: Transition into a weak, porous state upon setting Shrinkage upon setting [5] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Properties of an ideal sealer material: Ability to establish a fluid-tight seal Radiopaque (to be visible on radiographs) Antimicrobial Non-shrinking upon setting Non-staining Non-irritating to periapical tissues Insoluble in tissue fluid Biocompatible [1] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: The master cone is inserted into the canal to the working length, followed by the spreader, which, if properly selected, should reach 2 mm shorter. The spreader is then rotated and withdrawn from the canal, and the matching gutta-percha cone is inserted into the vacant space. [39] The process is repeated until the operator cannot insert the spreader deeper than the coronal third of the prepared canal. The excess gutta-percha is seared off with a heated instrument and compacted using a root canal plugger. [5] [41] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Glass Ionomer Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Improved adherence (via dentin bonding) Increased resistance to root fracture Enhanced resistance to solubility in tissue fluids Antimicrobial properties Sustained fluoride release [16] [17] Disadvantages: Technically difficult to prepare deep aspects of root canal (potentially limits dentin-bonding ability) Difficult to remove in cases of endodontic retreatment [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Calcium Hydroxide Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages Antimicrobial properties Milder cytotoxicity Promotes tissue healing [13] Disadvantages Solubility

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Instruments and Materials
retrieved_docs.content: The following instruments are used for the anterior access cavity: General instruments: Endodotic explorer, endodotic excavator, endodotic locking pliers, endodotic ruler, endodotic syringe, instrument organizer, transfer sponge, and instrument stopper. [38] [39] Intracanal instruments: Barbed broach, k-reamers, k-files (k-flex files and headstorm files), spreader, and plugger. [38] [40] Burs used for the anterior access cavity Access opening burs: round burs with 16mm bur shank. [38] [41] Access refining burs: Coarse flame-shaped burs, tapered round, diamonds to refine the wall. [38] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: Since there are numerous types of obturation materials, there are several ways to insert them into the root canal, and as a result, this section focuses primarily on the insertion technique of gutta-percha. Gutta-percha has 2 main obturation techniques, cold lateral condensation and warm vertical compaction. [5] [39] The cold lateral condensation technique involves the selection of a master gutta-percha cone, the use of spreaders, and the insertion of secondary gutta-percha cones. [5] [40] The master cone is a gutta-percha cone with a taper and diameter that matches the prepared root canal at the working length. This master cone is measured to the working length and inserted into the canal, where it displays slight resistance to removal, aptly referred to as "tug back" if the correct size cone is selected. [5] After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. At this point, a finger spreader or a hand spreader is pre-fit in the canal to reach within 1 to 2 mm short of the working length, and appropriately sized secondary gutta-percha cones are selected that closely match the size of the spreader. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Resin Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Improved adherence Enhanced tolerability of apical tissues [19] [20] Disadvantages: Technically difficult to prepare deep aspects of root canal (potentially limits dentin-bonding ability) Difficult to remove in cases of endodontic retreatment Potential for allergic and mutagenic bodily responses Shrinkage upon setting [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are few true contraindications for sealers and core obturation materials. One major contraindication is an allergy to any component of the material. [35] Allergies to constituent materials are rare but possible in every sealer and core material discussed, and the operator should take care to review all of a patient's possible allergies and be able to respond should a medical emergency arise. Other non-absolute contraindications are case-specific and depend on the treatment goal of the operator.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Glass Ionomer Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians advocating using glass ionomer sealers as an obturation material do so due to their dentin-bonding properties. [15] Dentin bonding results from the reaction between the polyacrylic acid in the glass ionomer and the hydroxyapatite on the dentinal wall and allows for excellent adhesion between the obturation material and the prepared canal wall. [16] Several major advantages arise from the improved adherence, including superior apical sealing, increased resistance to root fracture, and resistance to solubility in tissue fluids. [16] [17] Additional advantages include antimicrobial properties and sustained fluoride release. However, the dentinal walls are hard to reach with primer solutions in the middle and apical thirds, potentially limiting the adhesion of the sealer to the walls. [17] [18] Furthermore, the dentin-bonded glass ionomer sealer creates a homogenous solid material that is difficult to remove during endodontic retreatment. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Root canals are 3D structures with complex and unique anatomy; no 2 are alike. Achieving a 3D obturation with complete coronal, lateral, and apical seals is essential for long-term success. [1] Radiographs are still the main method used to visualize root canals during the diagnostic and treatment stages. However, one of their major weaknesses is that they only provide a 2D representation of 3D canals. [3] As a result, it is impossible to assess the quality of the canal seal created during the obturation stage. Introducing micro-CT imaging into the field of endodontics has dramatically enhanced the accuracy of diagnosis, cleaning shaping, and, ultimately, obturation. [4] There are countless materials and techniques for obturation. Most techniques employ a sealer and a core filling material to ensure complete obturation. This activity discusses available sealers and proceeds to core obturation materials.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Resin Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Resin sealers are non-eugenol sealers that provide excellent adhesion to the canal walls. [9] These sealers can be separated based on their constituent elements, such as epoxy resin-based or methacrylate resin-based sealers. Methacrylate resin-based sealers are further classified into 4 distinct generations, each with its unique formulation. The generations vary according to primer type and non-etching vs. self-etching capabilities. [9] [19] Resin-based sealers have several beneficial properties, including excellent adhesion and seal and good tolerability from apical tissues. [9] [19] However, it has also been shown to produce an initial inflammatory reaction with the potential for allergic and mutagenic responses. [20]
